summary: This interview with JP Kadarkarai, creator of FizzBee, explores the challenges of formal verification in distributed systems and why existing tools like TLA+ have failed to gain mainstream adoption. JP describes how his experience at Google with large-scale systems like GFS, Bigtable, and Spanner revealed the importance of formal verification, while his later work at Lyft and Clumio showed how the industry often adopts distributed architectures without theoretical foundations. FizzBee aims to make formal methods practical by using Python-like syntax instead of TLA+'s mathematical notation, automatically generating visualizations, and enabling model-based testing. The conversation also touches on how AI-assisted coding will increase the need for formal verification tools.
key_points: TLA+ adoption is hindered by its mathematical syntax and semantics that feel alien to most programmers, making it difficult to get team buy-in for spec reviews
FizzBee uses Python-like syntax to lower the barrier to entry, with the goal of making formal verification faster than writing traditional design documents
The microservices and NoSQL revolution has led many companies to adopt distributed architectures without solid theoretical foundations, causing consistency issues
FizzBee can automatically generate comprehensive tests from validated models, delivering TDD benefits without extra effort
As AI-assisted coding becomes prevalent, formal verification tools like FizzBee will be essential for validating that generated code behaves as intended
money_quotes: When there's a tradeoff between quality and time to market, time to market almost always takes precedence.
After leaving Google, I noticed a gap in the broader industry, where microservices and NoSQL databases were often adopted without a solid theoretical foundation.

The main reason was the complexity of TLA+'s syntax and semantics. Its syntax isn't intuitive—it relies heavily on ASCII representations of mathematical symbols, which can feel alien to most programmers.
The time taken to model a system in FizzBee is often less than the time it takes to write a traditional design document.
Fixing them with code changes like retries or locks didn't address the root cause—it often just moved the problem elsewhere.
As AI-assisted coding becomes more prevalent, validating that the code performs as intended will be essential for AI adoption.
key_insight: Formal verification will only achieve mainstream adoption when tools prioritize developer ergonomics over mathematical rigor, meeting engineers where they already are.
